coredata.dc:title: A highly water-soluble hydrogen-bond-induced emission carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent detection of water content in organic solvents
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Yanfei
Han, Bing
Liu, Xiao-Ting
Liu, Yuhao
Sun, Yuanqiang
Lu, Chao
coredata.dc:description: Water in organic solvents is a prevalent impurity that significantly influences chemical reactions and industrial processes. Carbon dots (CDs) gained attention as promising tools for chemosensing due to their advantageous characteristics, including easy synthesis, cost-effectiveness, and excellent adjustability and stability. However, limited solubility in water and turn off fluorescence response mode hinder the practical utilization of CDs for water sensing. To tackle such dilemma, a highly water-soluble CDs with distinctive hydrogen-bond-induced emission (HBIE) was rationally designed through introducing sulfone group into the widely employed p-phenylenediamine precursor. The inclusion of sulfone group imparts the CDs with notable water solubility, as well as distinctive ratiometric fluorescent response towards water content, exhibiting high sensitivity and selectivity. Upon exposure to water, the emission color of CDs undergoes a real-time transition from blue to yellow-green, which can be readily discerned by naked eyes. The CDs have been successfully applied in detecting water in commercially available alcohol. This study presents a straightforward yet efficacious approach for rationally design of CDs with unique HBIE characteristics and ratiometric fluorescent response, offering great potential for practical chemosensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Water content
Hydrogen-bond-induced emission
Ratiometric fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence-based detection of water in organic solvents
Hydrogen-bond induced emission of the TMP-CDs
Fluorescent response of TMP-CDs toward water
Water content assay in commercial ethanol

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U22A20397 , 22201257 , 22204148 and 21974008 ), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2023TQ0317 , 2023M743165 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), HRTEM image (b) and FT-IR spectra (c) of the TMP-CDs. XPS full-scan spectra (d) and high-resolution spectra of C1s (e), N1s (f), O1s (g), S2p (h) peaks of the TMP-CDs.
(a) Excitation and emission spectra of TMP-CDs (25 μg/mL) in PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) solution. (b) Excitation-dependent emission spectra of TMP-CDs, starting from 315 nm to 385 nm with a 10 nm increment.

Absorption (a) and emission (b) spectra of TMP-CDs (25 μg/mL) in different organic solvents and water under the excitation of 345 nm. (c) Photographs of TMP-CDs in different solvents under irradiation of 365 nm UV light.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of TMP-CDs (25 μg/mL) in the 1,4-dioxane with different amounts of water (λex = 345 nm). (b) Linear fitting relationship between fluorescence intensity of TMP-CDs (25 μg/mL) at 425 nm and water content from 30 to 90% (v/v). (c) Linear fitting relationship between fluorescence intensity of TMP-CDs (25 μg/mL) at 520 nm and water content from 0 to 80% (v/v). (d) Linear relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity of TMP-CDs at 425 nm and 520 nm and water content in the range of 0–40% (v/v). (e) Fluorescence spectra of EAP-CDs (25 μg/mL) in the 1,4-dioxane with different amounts of H2O (λex = 538 nm). (f) The relationship between maximum fluorescence intensity of EAP-CDs and water content. The inset shows the corresponding linear plot when the water content ranges from 10 to 60%. (g) Photographs of TMP-CDs (top) and EAP-CDs (bottom) in 1,4-dioxane/H2O system under irradiation of 365 nm UV light.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the TMP-CDs (25 μg/mL) in ethanol with different amount of water (λex = 345 nm). (b) The relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity of TMP-CDs at 425 nm and 520 nm and water content. The inset shows the corresponding linear plot when the water content ranges from 0 to 30% (v/v).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of TMP-CDs and the detection of water content in ethanol.
Determination of water contents in commercial ethanol.

head.ce:title: A highly water-soluble hydrogen-bond-induced emission carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent detection of water content in organic solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Green Catalysis Center and College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Green Catalysis Center and College of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Green Catalysis Center, and College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Green Catalysis Center and College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Green Catalysis Center and College of Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water in organic solvents is a prevalent impurity that significantly influences chemical reactions and industrial processes. Carbon dots (CDs) gained attention as promising tools for chemosensing due to their advantageous characteristics, including easy synthesis, cost-effectiveness, and excellent adjustability and stability. However, limited solubility in water and turn off fluorescence response mode hinder the practical utilization of CDs for water sensing. To tackle such dilemma, a highly water-soluble CDs with distinctive hydrogen-bond-induced emission (HBIE) was rationally designed through introducing sulfone group into the widely employed p-phenylenediamine precursor. The inclusion of sulfone group imparts the CDs with notable water solubility, as well as distinctive ratiometric fluorescent response towards water content, exhibiting high sensitivity and selectivity. Upon exposure to water, the emission color of CDs undergoes a real-time transition from blue to yellow-green, which can be readily discerned by naked eyes. The CDs have been successfully applied in detecting water in commercially available alcohol. This study presents a straightforward yet efficacious approach for rationally design of CDs with unique HBIE characteristics and ratiometric fluorescent response, offering great potential for practical chemosensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is one of the most essential and ubiquitous compounds in daily life, which is often hailed as the source of life. It is of crucial importance for the food, chemical, and pharmaceutical industries to detect and control water. For example, water is one of the most common pollutants in organic solvents. Many chemical reactions and devices are affected by water, such as organometallic chemistry and lithium-ion batteries [1,2]. Even trace amount of water can affect the results of the reactions or lead to catch fire and explosion. Therefore, the detection of water in organic solvents is critical in organic synthesis and chemical industry [3]. Currently, Karl Fischer titration is the most common method used to detect water content. However, the operation of the method is complicated and requires special experimental equipment and operation methods, which greatly limits its practical applications [4,5].

Fluorescent methods have been widely used in water detection because of its unique advantages of low cost, easy operation, high efficiency and sensitivity [6–8]. As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in biomedicine [9], chemosensing [10] and catalysis [11] due to their unique advantages such as facile synthesis, cost effectiveness, and high tenability and stability. Great efforts have been devoted in developing functional CDs for detecting water in recent years. For instance, Ye et al. reported a resorcinol derived-CDs for real-time monitoring of different contents of water in ethanol [12]. Wang et al. prepared red emissive CDs by using lignin and p-phenylenediamine as precursors for colorimetric and fluorescent detection of water in ethanol and acetone. Despite the considerable advancements, there are still significant drawbacks in this area. For example, most of the reported CDs showed a limited water solubility and fluorescent turn–off response towards water, leading to low sensitivity [13]. Many factors can influence the fluorescence measurement by a single fluorescence intensity change, including the local concentration of the CDs, changes of environments around the CDs, excitation and emission efficiency [14]. It is well known that ratiometric fluorescence measurements are able to avoid interference from factors compared to single-emission fluorescence detection [15]. Therefore, it is of great importance and challenge to design highly water-soluble CDs for ratiometric fluorescent detection of water.

Considering the fact that the fluorescence of most of the reported CDs could be quenched by water due to the strong polarity of the water, which increase the non-radiation transition through intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) or twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT), leading to low fluorescence quantum yield [16,17]. To block the water induced fluorescence quenching, unique strategies should be employed. It is well-known that the natural chromophore in green fluorescent protein showed high fluorescence due to the existence of hydrogen-bond network [18], which could stabilize the electronic states. Therefore, we envisioned that it is possible to develop highly fluorescent CDs in water through introducing functional groups that could form hydrogen-bond network with water.

Sulfone group is known as good hydrogen bond acceptor and hydrophilic group [19,20]. At the same time, the electron-withdrawing groups such as sulfone can effectively inhibiting the TICT process, significantly improving the quantum yield of fluorophores in aqueous solution, and enhancing the photostability of fluorophores [21,22]. Therefore, sulfone group was selected as the candidate. Phenylenediamine is among the most popular precursor for design of CDs and the phenylenediamine-derived CDs have shown excellent optical properties, and wide applications in chemosensing [23]. The amine group of phenylenediamine is easy to oxidation and polymerization, and the carbon dots is easily obtained by hydrothermal and solvothermal pathways [24]. Keep this consideration in mind, a highly water-soluble CDs for ratiometric fluorescent detection of water was developed by introducing sulfone group as the hydrogen bond acceptor into the p-phenylenediamine precursor [25]. Therefore, TMP-CDs was synthesized using 4-(4-aminophenyl)thiomorpholine-1,1-dioxide in ethanol solution by a one-step solvothermal method (Scheme 1 ). The TMP-CDs show blue fluorescence in aprotic solvents and yellow-green fluorescence in water. Based on this, the TMP-CDs can be used for the determination of water content in ethanol, which present ratio fluorescence with high accuracy and sensitivity.

4-(4-aminophenyl)thiomorpholine-1,1-dioxide and diethyl-1,4-phenylenediamine were purchased from Aladdin. Unless otherwise noted, other chemicals and reagents are purchased commercially and all chemical reagents are used without further purification.

Fluorescence spectra and UV–visible absorption spectra were determined by F-7100 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and TU-1810 spectrophotometer (Beijing, China), respectively. The carbon dots were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI-Tecnai G2, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were tested on a Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was tested on a Thermo Scientific (ESCALab250, USA).

The synthesis process was similar to the previous literature [26]. Briefly, 0.0559 g of 4-(4-aminophenyl)thiomorpholine-1,1-dioxide were mixed with 12 mL of ethanol (EtOH), and ultrasonic treatment for 5 min. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave, and reacted at a temperature of 180 °C for a duration of 12 h. Upon natural cooling to room temperature, the resulting brown mixture was purified using column chromatography employing a dichloromethane/methanol (20:1, v/v) mixture as the eluent. Similarly, the EPA-CDs were synthesized using a similar method, utilizing N,N-diethyl-1,4-phenylenediamine as the precursors, and the crude products were subsequently purified by silica column chromatography using ethyl acetate/petroleum (10:1, v/v) mixture as the eluent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence-based detection of water in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solution of TMP-CDs was prepared by dispersing the TMP-CDs in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). Typically, 5 μL of a 10 mg/mL TMP-CDs stock solution were added to the 1,4-dioxane/water mixture, resulting in samples with varying water content ranging from 0 to 100% (v/v) (total volume = 2 mL). The fluorescence emission spectra of the samples were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 345 nm. The same method was employed in the detection of water in DMSO and ethanol. Similarity, the 10 mg/ml of EPA-CDs stock solution was prepared in DMSO. Meanwhile, 5 μL of a 10 mg/mL EPA-CDs stock solution were added to the 1,4-dioxane/water mixture, resulting in samples with varying water content ranging from 0 to 100% (v/v) (total volume = 2 mL). The fluorescence emission spectra of the samples were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 538 nm.

The TMP-CDs were synthesized via solvothermal method, and TEM technique was employed to analyze their surface morphology and size. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the carbon dots exhibited a spherical shape and were evenly dispersed without noticeable aggregation. The particle size distribution ranged from 3.5 to 6.5 nm, with an average particle size of approximately 4.89 ± 0.35 nm. Furthermore, high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) revealed a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm in the CDs, which closely resembled that of graphite-carbon plane, thereby confirming the success of the synthesis process (Fig. 1b).

The surface state of TMP-CDs was analyzed using FT-IR spectra in order to investigate the characteristics of functional groups. The result is shown in Fig. 1c, where the broad and intense absorption band observed at 3493 cm−1 can be attributed to O–H/N–H bonds, while the relatively weaker absorption bands at 2927 cm−1 and 2854 cm−1 correspond to C–N bonds. The peaks observed at 1500 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 are indicative of the stretching vibration of C=C bonds within the benzene ring. Additionally, the absorption peaks of 1323 cm−1 and 1273 cm−1 provide the evidence for the presence of O=S=O bonds, and the sharp peak at 1502 cm−1 is associated with the C–N bonds. In addition, the chemical composition of the TMP-CDs was analyzed using XPS. The XPS full spectrum reveals that TMP-CDs predominantly consist of four elements, with respective contents of 87.81% C, 9.68% O, 3.87% N and 2.54% S (Fig. 1d). The C1s spectrum exhibits four peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.3 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.3 eV, corresponding to C=C/C–C, C–O/C–N, C–S, and C=O, respectively (Fig. 1e). The N1s spectrum displays three main peaks, which belong to amine N (398.4 eV), pyridine N (398.9 eV) and pyrrole N (287.9 eV) (Fig. 1f). As shown in Fig. 1g, the peaks at 532.1 eV and 533.8 eV on the O1s spectrum can be attributed to the presence of C=O/S=O and C–O/O–H, respectively. The S2p peak in Fig. 1h could be divided into two distinct peaks at 167.9 eV and 169.2 eV, corresponding to C–S and S=O, respectively. The results of XPS align with the findings from FT-IR experiments, suggesting that the TMP-CDs consist of C, N, O and S elements, and there with an abundance of sulfone functional groups on the TMP-CDs. The presence of sulfone functional groups contributes to the formation of hydrogen bond networks, enhancing the hydrophilicity of the TMP-CDs and enabling the detection of water in organic solvent.

The EPA-CDs characterization data are shown in Fig. S1. TEM images (Fig. S1a) showed that the prepared EPA-CDs was spherical and monodisperse, with an average size of 2.75 ± 0.32 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. S1a) revealed a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm in the CDs. The XPS full-scan spectrum (Fig. S1b) shows that EPA-CDs is composed of C (284.92 eV), O (532.01 eV), and N (399.50 eV) elements, and Fig. S1d for the N1s shows two peaks, 399.2 eV and 399.9 eV, which are composed of pyridine and amino-nitrogen.

The fluorescence spectra were used to study the optical properties of the carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the excitation and emission wavelengths of TMP-CDs in PBS solution were 345 nm and 520 nm, respectively. The excitation–dependent fluorescent spectra of TMP-CDs were then studied. As shown in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence emission wavelength remains stable when the excitation wavelength is increased from 315 nm to 385 nm at interval of 10 nm. When the excitation wavelength reaches 345 nm, the intensity of the fluorescence spectrum reaches its highest levels. Therefore, 345 nm and 520 nm are selected as the optimal excitation wavelength and emission wavelength respectively. In contrast, EPA-CDs exhibit optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 562 nm and 683 nm (Fig. S2a). Both TMP-CDs and EPA-CDs exhibit non-excitation dependence, which may be attributed to the presence of uniform emission states on its surface (Fig. 2b and Fig. S2b) [27,28].

The stability of carbon dots is a crucial factor in their practical application. To assess the optical stability of TMP-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of TMP-CDs in PBS solution was examined after continuous irradiation for 60 min. The results indicated that the fluorescence intensity of TMP-CDs did not undergo significant changes, demonstrating their high photostability (Fig. S3). Furthermore, the anti-interference capability of the TMP-CDs was further evaluated. In the presence of various metal ions and amino acid ions (500 μM), the fluorescence intensity exhibited minimal alterations, suggesting that these interferences could be disregarded in the PBS solution (Fig. S4). In addition, the pH dependence of carbon dots was investigated. As shown in Fig. S5 and Fig. S6, there is no marked change in fluorescence intensity with the pH range of 2–12, which prove that TMP-CDs have excellent pH stability. This can be attributed to the presence of sulfone groups, which facilitated the establishment of a hydrogen bond network, thereby conferring the carbon dots with remarkable light stability and excellent acid-base resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen-bond induced emission of the TMP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the TMP-CDs in different organic solvents and water were then examined. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the main absorption wavelengths of TMP-CDs in different organic solvents and water located around 350 nm. To our delight, the TMP-CDs showed strong yellow-green fluorescence in water with a main emission band at 520 nm. By contrast, a robust blue fluorescence was observed when TMP-CDs were in organic solutions, including 1,4-dioxane, dichloromethane, ethyl acetate, tetrahydrofuran, ethanol, acetonitrile, N,N-dimethylformamide and acetone (Fig. 3b and c). The large difference of TMP-CDs emission behavior in water and organic solvents indicates that TMP-CDs has typical hydrogen-bond induced emission (HBIE). The absorption and emission spectra of the contrast carbon dots EPA-CDs were also examined. As shown in Figs. S7b and S7c, the main emission wavelength of EPA-CDs gradually exhibits a red-shifted tendency from 599 nm to 683 nm with the polarity increase, and a very low fluorescence was observed in water, which is in agreement with the typical solvatochroimic effect of carbon dots and fluorophores [29,30]. This unique HBIE phenomenon could be attributed to the strong hydrogen acceptor ability of the sulfone group, which could form a hydrogen-bond network with the water and inhibit the non-radiation transition, leading to the robust yellow-green emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of TMP-CDs toward water
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their notable HBIE effect, the TMP-CDs were used for detecting water in aprotic organic solvents. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the emission spectra of the TMP-CDs exhibited a gradual redshift from 405 nm to 425 nm as the water content in 1, 4-dioxane increased. When the water content was higher than 50%, the unique HBIE band at 520 nm was emerged. Furthermore, the water sensing capabilities of TMP-CDs in 1,4-dioxane with varying water contents under UV light are shown in Fig. 4g. The emission color of TMP-CDs gradually transitioned from blue to bright yellow-green with the increase of water contents. The maximum fluorescence intensity at 425 nm was negatively correlated with water content (R2 = 0.9659) in the range of 30–90% (Fig. 4b). Meanwhile, the maximum fluorescence intensity at 520 nm was positively correlated with water content (R2 = 0.9802) in the range of 0–80% (Fig. 4c). A calibration curve was constructed using the ratio of TMP-CDs fluorescence intensity at 425 nm and 520 nm (Fig. 4d). The linear range of the curve can be observed in 1,4-dioxane, spams from 0 to 40% (R2 = 0.9879). In contrast, the fluorescence response of the EAP-CDs toward water does not remain consistent under the same conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 4e, the fluorescence intensity of EAP-CDs gradually decreases and exhibits a gradual redshift from 599 nm to 683 nm with the increase of water contents without any novel emission band emerged. There was a negative correlation between the maximum fluorescence intensity of EAP-CDs and water content ranging from 10% to 60% (Fig. 4f, insets). Obviously, the emission color of EAP-CDs showed a quenching trend with the increase of water contents (Fig. 4g). By analyzing the varied compositions of the carbon dots and their distinct sensing characteristics, it becomes evident that the introducing of sulfone functional groups enables the attainment of ratiometric fluorescent detection of water. The hydrophobic and strong electron-donating nature of the diethylamine group may lead to a reduction in water solubility of the EAP-CDs, consequently resulting in fluorescence quenching. Conversely, the sulfone group on the TMP-CDs facilitates the formation of hydrogen bond network with water, and achieving ratiometric fluorescence detection of water. Similar results were also observed for the TMP-CDs in DMSO (Fig. S8).

Both ethanol and water are considered protic solvents capable of forming hydrogen bonds. It is of great significance and challenge to develop probes that can accurately measure water content in ethanol. However, as described above, TMP-CDs showed blue fluorescence in ethanol that similar with aprotic solvents such as DMSO and acetonitrile. This may be due to the fact that the hydrogen bond donating ability of the ethanol is weaker than that of water, which could not form effective hydrogen-bond network to inhibit the non-irradiation transition and induce red-shift emission [31,32]. Therefore, we utilize the HBIE properties of the TMP-CDs to detect water in ethanol. As shown in Fig. 5 , with the increase of water contents, the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm gradually decreased, while that at 520 nm showed a trend of first enhancement and then decrease (Fig. 5a). A calibration curve was constructed using the ratio of TMP-CDs fluorescence intensity at 425 nm and 520 nm (Fig. 5b). The linear range of the curve can be observed in ethanol, spams from 0 to 30% (R2 = 0.9879). The limits of detection (LOD) were determined based on the detection limit equation of LOD = 3σ/b (b is the curve slope; σ is the standard deviation of blank samples) [33]. The LOD for detecting water in ethanol was calculated to be 0.54%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water content assay in commercial ethanol
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practical utility of the ratiometric fluorescence carbon dots for detecting water contents in ethanol, we employed three commercially available 95% ethanol samples from different manufacturers. The TMP-CDs were individually dissolved in the samples respectively and the water contents were determined. As shown in Table 1 , the average recovery rate of water in ethanol samples were ranged from 99.6%∼107.5%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) is below 3%, indicating that TMP-CDs possess the potential for quantitative detection of real-world samples.

In summary, this manuscript presents the incorporation of sulfone group as a hydrogen bond acceptor into carbon dots, resulting in the synthesis of ratiometric fluorescent carbon dots with hydrogen bond induced fluorescence characteristic through a solvothermal method. The introduction of the sulfone functional group in TMP-CDs leads to hydrogen-bond induced emission effects, which realize the ratiometric fluorescent detection of water contents in ethanol with low detection limit, and promising outcomes have been obtained when analyzing real samples. The design strategy provided here was hoped to inspire the development of more efficient functional carbon dots for chemosensing applications.

Yanfei Jia: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Bing Han: Writing - review & editing. Xiao-Ting Liu: Investigation, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Yuhao Liu: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Yuanqiang Sun: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Chao Lu: Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U22A20397, 22201257, 22204148 and 21974008), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023TQ0317, 2023M743165).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of water content on the Kinetics of p -xylene liquid-phase catalytic oxidation to terephthalic acid
Ind. Eng. Chem. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Wang, X. Li, L. Wang, Y. Cheng, G. Xie, Effect of Water Content on the Kinetics of p -Xylene Liquid-Phase Catalytic Oxidation to Terephthalic Acid, Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 44 (2005) 4518-4522. https://doi.org/10.1021/ie048755s.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of trace water in an organic solvent based on bimetallic lanthanide metal–organic frameworks
